coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots anchored NiO/Co3O4 ultrathin nanosheets as advanced cathodes for hybrid supercapacitors
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Zhenyuan
Liu, Kai
Li, Na
Zhang, Hongyan
Dai, Wenyao
Shen, Xiaoping
Zhu, Guoxing
Kong, Lirong
Yuan, Aihua
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we demonstrate an advanced cathode of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) anchored NiO/Co3O4 ultrathin nanosheets for hybrid supercapacitors by a facile hydrothermal-calcination route. Owing to the well defined thin-plate structure and ternary composition, the optimized NiO/Co3O4/NCDs nanosheets demonstrate a high specific capacity of 976.3 C g−1 (1775 F g−1) at 1 A g−1, and a splendid cycling stability of approximately 95.7% retention over 10,000 continuous cycles (15 A g−1). In addition, a hybrid supercapacitor is constructed by using NiO/Co3O4/NCDs nanosheets as cathode and reduced graphene oxide (RGO) supported NCDs composites as anode. The obtained NiO/Co3O4/NCDs//RGO/NCDs hybrid supercapacitor delivers a maximum energy density of 41.6 Wh kg−1, together with outstanding cycling stability (no decay after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1). Therefore, the ultrathin sheet-like structured NiO/Co3O4/NCDs cathode presents a great potential for supercapacitor application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nickel oxide
Cobalt oxide
Carbon dots
Nanosheet
Hybrid supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NiO/NCDs and Co3O4/NCDs composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of NiO/NCDs-2 and NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites.
SEM images of (a) NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-1, (b) NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-2, (c) NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 and (d) NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-4 composites.
(a-c) TEM and (d) high-resolution TEM images of the NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 composites.
Elemental mapping images of the NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 nanosheets.
High resolution XPS spectra of (a) Ni 2p, (b) Co 2p, (c) O 1s and (d) N 1s of the NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 nanosheets.

(a) CV curves of NiO/NCDs-2, Co3O4/NCDs and NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites at 25 mV s−1; (b) Charge and discharge profiles of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 composites at 1–20 A g−1; (c) Charge and discharge curves of NiO/NCDs-2, Co3O4/NCDs and NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites at 1 A g−1; (d) Specific capacities of NiO/NCDs-2, Co3O4/NCDs and NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites as a function of the current density.

(a) CV curves of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3//RGO/NCDs hybrid supercapacitor device at different scan rates; (b) Charge-discharge profiles of the device at various current densities; (c) Ragone plots of the device; (d) Cycling stability of the device at 10 A g−1.
Comparison of the NiO/Co3O4/NCDs//RGO/NCD device with previously reported hybrid supercapacitors.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots anchored NiO/Co3O4 ultrathin nanosheets as advanced cathodes for hybrid supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we demonstrate an advanced cathode of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) anchored NiO/Co3O4 ultrathin nanosheets for hybrid supercapacitors by a facile hydrothermal-calcination route. Owing to the well defined thin-plate structure and ternary composition, the optimized NiO/Co3O4/NCDs nanosheets demonstrate a high specific capacity of 976.3 C g−1 (1775 F g−1) at 1 A g−1, and a splendid cycling stability of approximately 95.7% retention over 10,000 continuous cycles (15 A g−1). In addition, a hybrid supercapacitor is constructed by using NiO/Co3O4/NCDs nanosheets as cathode and reduced graphene oxide (RGO) supported NCDs composites as anode. The obtained NiO/Co3O4/NCDs//RGO/NCDs hybrid supercapacitor delivers a maximum energy density of 41.6 Wh kg−1, together with outstanding cycling stability (no decay after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1). Therefore, the ultrathin sheet-like structured NiO/Co3O4/NCDs cathode presents a great potential for supercapacitor application.

ce:section.ce:para: With the gradual consumption of fossil fuel, the development of energy storage devices has become global concerns [1,2]. As one of the prime choices, supercapacitors have elicited a great scientific interest by virtue of their desirable power density and fast charge–discharge rate [3–5]. Whereas, the poor energy density of supercapacitors severely hinder their use in energy storage systems [6–8]. Accordingly, numerous efforts have been dedicated to improve the energy density of supercapacitors without decreasing the power density of them. More recently, hybrid supercapacitors consisting of a capacitive electrode and a battery-like electrode are able to deliver much higher energy density than conventional supercapacitors due to the larger specific capacities of the battery-type materials [9–11]. Transition metal oxides are demonstrated to be superior battery-type electrode materials on account of their naturtal abundance and environmental friendliness [12]. Among these materials, NiO with ultrahigh theoretical capacity and Co3O4 with long cycling life have elicited the extensive interest as redox-active electrodes for supercapacitors [13–15]. Compared with the corresponding individual constituents, the effective combination of NiO and Co3O4 could offer competitive advantages for the increment of electrochemical activity [16]. However, the major obstacles for NiO/Co3O4 composites are their low specific surface area and poor electrical conductivity owing to their semiconductor nature. These disadvantages would lead to a lower specific capacity of NiO/Co3O4 composites and severely confine their further development in supercapacitors.

In order to overcome the above obstacles, integrating conductive carbon and constructing nanostructured materials are considered as plausible methods to optimize the electrochemical properties of NiO/Co3O4 [1,17]. Recently, carbon dots, as a new type of zero-dimensional carbonaceous materials, have become the focus in the field of basic research owing to their diverse physicochemical properties and beneficial attributes, such as low production cost, good dispersion in solvent, and excellent surface grafting ability [18,19]. It is found that carbon dots can provide a favorable interface for the interaction between electrolyte and electrodes, thus improving the specific capacities and cycle stabilities of the electrode materials. The emergence of carbon dots have exhibit exciting prospects in the energy conversion and storage fields [20]. On the other hand, attributing to the higher specific surface area, more exposed active sites and larger degree of anisotropy, ultrathin two-dimensional nanostructures offer superior charge storage characteristics for the electrode materials and have attracted increasing interest in electrochemical energy storage systems [21,22]. Unfortunately, few study has been reported on carbon dots anchored NiO/Co3O4 two-dimensional nanostructures for energy storage applications. Herein, we developed an advanced cathode of ultrathin NiO/Co3O4 nanosheets supported nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) for hybrid supercapacitors. The NiO/Co3O4/NCDs ultrathin nanosheets were successfully synthetized through a hydrothermal-calcination two-step strategy, which realize simultaneous boosts in specific capacity (976.3 C g−1, 1 A g−1) and cycle stability (95.7% retention, 10,000 cycles). Meanwhile, a corresponding hybrid supercapacitor device based on NiO/Co3O4/NCDs ultrathin nanosheets and reduced graphene oxide (RGO)/NCDs shows a high energy density of 41.6 Wh kg−1 at 800 W kg−1 with excellent cycling stability (no decay after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1), suggesting the high application potential of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs ultrathin nanosheets for energy storage systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NiO/NCDs and Co3O4/NCDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The chemicals were of analytical level and used without further purification. The NCDs were fabricated by a hydrothermal method according to our previous work [23]. For the preparation of NiO/NCDs composites, NCDs powder and Ni(Ac)2·4H2O (0.5 g) were dissolved in 35 mL of deionized water. The mixed solution was stirred for 30 min and then transferred to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) autoclave. After reaction at 180 °C for 10 h, the precursor was washed with deionized water and ethanol, and then vacuum-dried at 50 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the solids were calcined at 350 °C for 2 h in argon gas ambience. The samples were labeled as NiO, NiO/NCDs-1, NiO/NCDs-2 and NiO/NCDs-3 with varying the mass of NCDs (0, 2, 4 and 6 mg), respectively. As a comparison, Co3O4/NCDs composites were also synthesized by the same process as NiO/NCDs-2.

The NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites were synthesized by the same procedure as that of NiO/NCDs-2, and labeled as NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-1, NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-2, NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 and NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-4 by varying the mass ratios of Ni(Ac)2·4H2O and Co(Ac)2·4H2O of 4:1, 1:1 and 2:3 and 1:4, respectively.

The crystalline nature of the composites was analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance). Raman analysis (horiba evolution) was carried out with 532 nm laser light. The composition of the composites was evaluated by inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES, Vista-MXP, Varian). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed by a Thermo ESCALAB 250 XI spectrometer. N2 adsorption–desorption experiments were conducted on a micrometrics ASAP 2020 analyzer. The microstructure of the products was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7800F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100F). Elemental mapping characterization was carried out on JEM 2100F microscope.

The electrochemical characteristics were conducted using a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation with 3.0 M KOH solution as electrolyte. For three-electrode experiments, a Hg/HgO electrode and a platinum plate were used as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. For electrochemical characterizations of the NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites, NiO/Co3O4/NCDs (80 wt%), acetylene carbon (10 wt%) and PTFE binder (10 wt%) were thoroughly mixed in ethanol. The obtained paste was spreaded on a pretreated nickel foam (1 × 3 cm2) and pressed at 10 Mpa. Subsequently, the nickel foam (containing approximately 2 mg of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs) was heated at 60 °C for 12 h. The NiO/Co3O4/NCDs//RGO/NCDs supercapacitor device was constructed in a 2032 type coin cell with a NKK MPF30AC non-woven membrane as separator. The mass loading of electrodes for as-assembled hybrid supercapacitor was approximately 7.6 mg. The RGO/NCDs anode was fabricated according to our previous study [24].

The representative morphologies of bare NiO, NiO/NCDs and NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites are presented in Figs. S2 and 2 . The bare NiO exhibits a platelet-like structure. However, the size and shape of the platelets are irregular. After combination with NCDs, these platelets are newly assembled into a flower-like architecture. It is known that the NCDs are negatively charged due to the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface. When NCDs were added to the reaction system, the electrostatic interaction between the Ni2+ ions and NCDs could offer a driving force for the assembly of NiO nuclei around the NCDs to form a tightly packed flower structure. The flower structure of NiO/NCDs becomes regular and uniform in size with the increase of the content of NCDs, which is consistent with previous studies [28,29]. The SEM images of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites are illustrated in Fig. 2, from which we can observed that the composites all display sheet-like shape. With increasing Co3O4 content, NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites become an ultrathin graphene-like architecture, and the wrinkles on the sheets can be clearly observed. This graphene-like structure is expected to optimize the electrochemical characteristics of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs due to its larger contactable surface area and more revealing active sites. The morphology of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites was further investigated by TEM (Fig. 3 ), which clearly display the transparent feature of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs, suggesting an ultrathin nature. The high-magnification TEM images in Fig. 3b and c confirm that the NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 ultrathin nanosheets are composed of a large amount of nanoparticles. Meanwhile, abundant pores can be observed on the nanosheets. This porous architecture is beneficial to the penetration of the electrolyte and plays a positive role in the electrochemical properties of the electrode materials [30]. The NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 ultrathin nanosheets deliver a specific surface area of ca. 108.4 m2 g−1 and the main pore size is about 4.1 nm (Fig. S3). In the high-resolution TEM image of the NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 nanosheets (Fig. 3d), the measured lattice fringes of 0.15 and 0.25 nm match well with the (2 2 0) lattice plane of NiO and (3 1 1) plane of Co3O4, respectively. In addition, a 0.22 nm lattice fringe was also detected in Fig. 3d, which correspond to the (1 0 0) crystal plane of NCDs. These results reveal that the ternary NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites have been successfully prepared. In order to investigate the distribution of the elements in the ternary composites, elemental mapping analysis of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 was also carried out. As shown in Fig. 4 , the C, N, O, Ni and Co elements show a highly uniform distribution, which further demonstrate the successful preparation of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs ternary composites and the three substances are homogeneously distributed on the nanosheets.

The composition and the chemical states of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 nanosheets were conducted by XPS (Fig. 5 ). The Ni 2p spectrum in Fig. 5a reveals two spin–orbit doublets (Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2) and two satellite peaks (signed as “Sat.”). Due to the surface effects, the Ni 2p3/2 can be splited into two peaks located at about 854.3 and 856 eV [31]. The binding energy at 873.0 eV is attributed to the Ni 2p1/2 peak. The two satellite peaks can be observed at 861.5 and 879.7 eV, which indicates the presence of NiO in the composites [32]. In Fig. 5b, the bands at 780.0 eV and 795.5 eV correspond to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, which can be fitted into two peaks attributing to the characteristic of Co2+ and Co3+ in Co3O4 [33,34]. Meanwhile, the peaks at 786.7 eV and 803.0 eV correspond to their vibrating satellite peaks [35]. The XPS spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 6 c) presents the fitting peaks at around 529.6 and 531.2 eV associating with the Ni–O and Co–O bonds. The N1s spectrum is shown in Fig. 5d, further indicating the presence of NCDs in the ternary composites.

The electrochemical characteristics of bare NiO, NiO/NCDs and NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites are conducted with a three-electrode apparatus. From Fig. S4, it can be clearly seen that the NCDs have a notable impact on the electrochemical properties of NiO. The NiO/NCDs-2 electrode has a specific capacity of 424.1 C g−1 at 1 A g−1, which is more than twice the specific capacity of bare NiO electrode. The enhanced electrochemical properties may be ascribed to the brilliant properties of the NCDs and distinctive flower-like architecture of NiO/NCDs composites [28,36]. Fig. 6a presents the cycle voltammetry (CV) curves of NiO/NCDs-2, Co3O4/NCDs and NiO/Co3O4/NCDs at 25 mV s−1. All CV profiles are almost symmetrical, indicating that the composite electrodes have good reversibility. Meanwhile, the CV curves of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs ternary composites have larger closed areas than NiO/NCDs-2 and Co3O4/NCDs, which suggests that NiO/Co3O4/NCDs electrodes deliver enhanced energy storage capacities and the NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 electrode has the largest specific capacity among all the composites. The redox reactions of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs electrodes can be described as follows:
NiO + OH− → NiOOH + e−(1)
Co3O4 + OH− + H2O → 3CoOOH + e−(2)
CoOOH + OH− → CoO2 + H2O + e−(3)

The charge–discharge profiles of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 electrode collected at various current densities are displayed in Fig. 6b. The charge–discharge curves exhibit voltage platforms, further suggesting that the energy storage mechanism of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 composites is primarily based on the redox reaction. Meanwhile, all NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites exhibit longer discharging times and wider voltage platforms at 1 A g−1 (Fig. 6c), indicating that the NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites have superior electrochemical properties over NiO/NCDs-2 and Co3O4/NCDs. This result is consistent with the CV measurements. The improved electrochemical characteristics of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs than NiO/NCDs and Co3O4/NCDs could be ascribed to the synergistic effect between NiO and Co3O4 [37,38]. It is known that NiO has been extensive studied as a redox-active electrode material for supercapacitors due to its high theoretical capacity. However, NiO electrode has the drawback of low ion/electron transfer, which results in low energy efficiency and poor rate capability. By contrast, Co3O4 electrode has the merit of fast ion insert/emergence rate. Binary NiO/Co3O4 exhibit higher electronic conductivity than the single counterparts. On the other hand, the presence of Co3O4 with multiple oxidation states (Co2+, Co3+ and Co4+) grants the NiO electrode with rich redox reactions and brings extra capacity [16]. The specific capacities of NiO/NCDs-2 and NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites at various current densities are estimated from the discharging times and presented in Fig. 6d. It is obviously that the NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 composites deliver the highest specific capacities among all the electrode materials. At current densities of 1, 2, 5, 10, 15 and 20 A g−1, the specific capacities are calculated to be about 976.3 (1775 F g−1), 885.5 (1610 F g−1), 859.7 (1563 F g−1), 809.6 (1472 F g−1), 761.8 (1385 F g−1) and 700.2 (1273 F g−1) C g−1, respectively. Cycling stability is one of the most important factors in evaluating the electrochemical properties of electrode materials. As shown in Fig. S5, the specific capacity of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 at 15 A g−1 decreases slightly within 6000 cycles and preserves approximately 95.7% after 10,000 consecutive cycles, demonstrating the excellent long-term cycle stability of our proposed NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 electrode. The slightly increase of the specific capacity could be due to more complete intercalation and de-intercalation of electrolytes after 6000th cycles, which helps to create rich electroactive sites for fast redox reaction and allows the trapped ions to gradually diffuse out [39–41]. Moreover, the structural stability of the NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 nanosheets after the cycling test was also checked by SEM observation (Fig. S6). The structure of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 does not display noticeable changes, which further confirms the high stability of our prepared NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites. The electrochemical properties of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 ultrathin nanosheets are comparable to previously reported Ni/Co mixed oxides [36,38,42–44]. The improved electrochemical properties can be attributed to the graphene-like porous architecture and distinctive ternary components.

In order to evaluate the practical application of our proposed ternary composites, a hybrid supercapacitor was constructed by choosing NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3 ultrathin nanosheets as positive electrode and RGO/NCDs composites as negative electrode. The mass ratio of RGO/NCDs to NiO/Co3O4/NCDs was calculated to be about 2.8 according to the charge balance [45]. Fig. 7 a displays the CV curves of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3//RGO/NCDs at various scan rates. No obvious irreversible current was observed, which suggests that the operating voltage of the device can be enlarged to 1.6 V. Moreover, all CV curves present a nearly quasi-rectangular shapes. This demonstrates that NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3//RGO/NCDs shows the contribution mainly from capacitance [46]. At the same time, the shape of these CV curves remains almost unchanged as the sweep rate increases, indicating the high reversibility and fast electron transport of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3//RGO/NCDs. The constant current charge–discharge profiles of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3//RGO/NCDs were analyzed at 1–20 A g−1 (Fig. 7b). The nearly symmetrical profile of these curves suggests the high reversibility of the device [47]. As can be seen from the Ragone plots in Fig. 7c, the NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3//RGO/NCDs device delivers a maximum energy density of 41.6 Wh kg−1 at 800 W kg−1 and retains a energy density of 21.3 Wh kg−1 even at 16000 W kg−1. To evaluate the long-term cycle stability of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3//RGO/NCDs, the device was subjected to charge–discharge tests for 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1. As presented in Fig. 7d, the capacity of the hybrid supercapacitor increases gradually with the increase of charge–discharge times. No decay of the capacity can be found after 10,000 cycles, indicating the outstanding cycle stability of the hybrid supercapacitor device. Furthermore, as shown in Table 1 , the electrochemical characteristics of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs-3//RGO/NCDs are comparable with those of previously reported hybrid supercapacitors. These results demonstrate the great potential of our hybrid supercapacitor device for commercial reality in modern electronics.

To sum up, the NiO/Co3O4/NCDs ternary composites have been successfully prepared by an effective hydrothermal-calcination two-step strategy. An ultrathin graphene-like porous architecture can be generated after combination Co3O4 with NiO/NCDs composites. Benefitting from the particular structure and ternary composition, the NiO/Co3O4/NCDs composites possess an enhanced specific capacity (976.3 C g−1 at 1 A g−1) and a splendid cycling stability (4.3% loss after 10,000 cycles). The hybrid supercapacitor device constructed with NiO/Co3O4/NCDs cathode and RGO/NCDs anode achieves a satisfactory energy density of 41.6 Wh kg−1. More importantly, no decay of the capacity can be found after 10,000 cycles at high current density of 10 A g−1. The outstanding electrochemical properties of NiO/Co3O4/NCDs ultrathin nanosheets show the bright prospects for advanced energy storage devices.

Zhenyuan Ji: Writing - original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Kai Liu: Investigation, Validation. Na Li: Methodology, Validation. Hongyan Zhang: Validation. Wenyao Dai: Investigation, Software. Xiaoping Shen: Writing - review & editing, Methodology. Guoxing Zhu: Writing - review & editing. Lirong Kong: Formal analysis, Software. Aihua Yuan: Formal analysis, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51602129 and 21875091) and the Young Talents Training Program of Jiangsu University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2020.06.070.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
